titleStmt.title: Letter from Lord Newton to the Under Secretary of Ireland, 19 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Wodehouse Legh

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Thomas Wodehouse Legh, 2nd Baron Newton, (1857-1942) to the Under Secretary of Ireland, Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938) referring to correspondence from Mrs Manning concerning the repayment of a sum from the British Relief Funds. Enclosed with the letter is a statement explaining Mrs Manning’s inability to repay the sum due to her son’s enlistment in the army and her failure to receive a dependent’s allowance. In recognition of the service of several of her sons Mrs Manning hopes that Newton will consider the debt repaid. In the letter Mrs Manning's youngest son was noted as being a member of the 'famous Pals Battalion which...suffered so much last August in the Dardanelles.' Said Pals Battalion were the members of D company, 7th Battalion, Royal Dublin Fusiliers. Referred to as the 'Toffs in the Toughs,' many of their volunteers came from the Irish rugby team, and the students and staff of Trinity college. They suffered heavy casualties during the August offensive at Suvla Bay, Gallipoli.In 1916 Lord Newton became assistant under secretary of state for foreign affairs, and was put in charge of two departments at the Foreign Office, one dealing with foreign propaganda and the other with prisoners of war. Sir Robert Chalmers was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell (1871-1941), his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
FOREIGN OFFICE 19 JUL 1916 Sir:â I am directed by Secretary Sir E. Grey to
transmit herewith a copy of a letter which has been
received from Mrs. Manning of 4, Pembroke Road,
Dublin, in response to a request by this Department
for repayment of a sum advanced to her daughter
while the latter was in Germany from British Relief
Funds held at the disposal of the United States
Ambassador at Berlin. Before deciding as to what course should be
adopted in regard to the recovery of the sum in
question, I am to request that Sir E. Grey may be
informed as to whether the facts set forth by Mrs.
Manning are correct. I am
Sir,
Your most obedient
humble Servant, Newton The Under Secretary Irish Office.
Copy
(83688/14/P) 4, PEMBROKE ROAD, DUBLIN. Mrs. Manning's compliments to the Under â
Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs, begs to
state she cannot send a further instalment at
present, her eldest son from whom she got her chief
support has joined the Army and is now in the 2nd
South African Field Ambulance stationed at
Kilindini, British East Africa, when he joined last
December he advised her she should (as a dependent)
get a seperation allowance since that time she
has never received a penny which leaves her in
great want of means and quite unable to meet her
engagements at present. Mrs. Manning now has two sons fighting for
King and Country her youngest son having joined the
Army in September 1914 and is one the few
survivors of the famous Pals Battalion which
Battalion went through so much, and suffered so much
last August in the Dardanelles. Mrs. Manning thinks under existing circumstances
and in consideration of her want of means it would
be a very gracious act on the part of the Under
Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs to consider
the debt paid. The Under Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs.
